content: Two point objects of masses 1.5 g and 2.5 g respectively are at a distance of 16 cm apart, the centre of gravity is at a distance x from the object of mass 1.5 gm where x is
explanation:
Taking the moment of forces about centre of gravity G (1.5)g x = 2.5g (16 $$-$$ x)

$$\Rightarrow$$ 3x = 80 $$-$$ 5x

or, 8x = 80

or, x = 10 cm

content: The position vector of a particle of mass $$\mathrm{m}$$ moving with a constant velocity $$\vec{v}$$ is given by $$\vec{r}=x(t) \hat{i}+b \hat{j}$$, where $$\mathrm{b}$$ is a constant. At an instant, $$\vec{r}$$ makes an angle $$\theta$$ with the $$x$$-axis as shown in the figure. The variation of the angular momentum of the particle about the origin with $$\theta$$ will be
explanation:
$\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+b \hat{j}$

$\begin{aligned} & \vec{v}=\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t}=\frac{d x}{d t} \hat{i}=v \hat{i} \\\\ & \vec{r} \times \vec{v}=b v(-\hat{k}) \\\\ & \vec{r} \times m \vec{v}=b m v(-\hat{k}) \\\\ & |\vec{L}|=b m v \quad(\vec{v} \text { constant })\end{aligned}$

content: A small ball of mass m is suspended from the ceiling of a floor by a string of length $$\mathrm{L}$$. The ball moves along a horizontal circle with constant angular velocity $$\omega$$, as shown in the figure. The torque about the centre (O) of the horizontal circle is
explanation: Net force $=\mathrm{T} \sin \theta$ along $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PO}}$ $\Rightarrow$ Torque about $\mathrm{O}$ is zero.

content: A small sphere of mass m and radius r slides down the smooth surface of a large hemispherical bowl of radius R. If the sphere starts sliding from rest, the total kinetic energy of the sphere at the lowest point $$\mathrm{A}$$ of the bowl will be [given, moment of inertia of sphere $$=\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{mr}^2$$]
explanation:
After we will apply the concept of energy conservation, as the surface of the bowl is smooth, small sphere does not roll as there is no friction.

So, small sphere does not gain rotational kinetic energy.

Hence, by energy conservation,

$$\Delta KE$$ Loss in PE

$$K{E_{{f^{ }}}} - 0 = mgR - mg(r)$$

$$ \Rightarrow K{E_f} = mg(R - r)$$

Hence, total kinetic energy of the spheres at the lowest point A is

$$KE = mg(R - r)$$

content: A mouse of mass m jumps on the outside edge of a rotating ceiling fan of moment of inertia I and radius R. The fractional loss of angular velocity of the fan as a result is,
explanation:
By conservation of angular momentum,

$$\begin{aligned} & I \omega=\left(I+M R^2\right) \cdot \omega^{\prime} \\ \therefore & \quad\frac{\omega^{\prime}}{\omega}=\frac{I}{I+m R^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ The fractional loss of angular velocity is

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\omega-\omega^{\prime}}{\omega} & =1-\frac{\omega^{\prime}}{\omega}=1-\frac{I}{I+m R^2} \\ & =\frac{m R^2}{I+m R^2} \end{aligned}$$

content: A uniform rod of length L pivoted at one end P is freely rotated in a horizontal plane with an angular velocity $$\omega$$ about a vertical axis passing through P. If the temperature of the system is increased by $$\Delta$$T, angular velocity becomes $${\omega  \over 2}$$. If coefficient of linear expansion of the rod is $$\alpha$$($$\alpha$$ << 1), then $$\Delta$$T will be
explanation:
Initial length of rod = L

Initial angular velocity, $$\omega$$i = $$\omega$$

When rod pivoted at one end is freely rotated in horizontal plane, then its angular momentum,

Li = I1$$\omega$$1

$${L_i} = \left( {{{M{L^2}} \over 3}} \right).\,\omega $$


When temperature of the system is increased with temperature $$\Delta$$T, then change in length of rod $$\Delta$$L is given as

$$\Delta$$L = $$\alpha$$L$$\Delta$$T

Now, new length of the rod,

L' = $$\Delta$$L + L = $$\alpha$$L$$\Delta$$T + L = L($$\alpha$$$$\Delta$$T + 1)

$$\Rightarrow$$ L' = L(1 + $$\alpha$$$$\Delta$$T) ....... (i)

Now, new angular momentum,

Lf = I2$$\omega$$2 = $${{{ML{'^2}} \over 3}.{\omega \over 2}}$$

By the law of conservation of angular momentum,

Lf = Li

I2$$\omega$$2 = $${{{M{L^2}} \over 3}.\,\omega }$$

$${{{ML{'^2}} \over 3}.{\omega \over 2}}$$ = $${{{M{L^2}} \over 3}.\,\omega }$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{L{'^2}} \over 2} = {L^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{L^2}{{(1 + \alpha \Delta T)}^2}} \over 2} = {L^2}$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$ \Rightarrow {(1 + \alpha \Delta T)^2} = 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + {\alpha ^2}\Delta {T^2} + 2\alpha \Delta T = 2$$

Since, $$\alpha$$ is very small, hence the term $$\alpha$$2$$\Delta$$T2 may be neglected.

$$\therefore$$ $$1 + 2\alpha \Delta T = 2 \Rightarrow 2\alpha \Delta T = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow \Delta T = {1 \over {2\alpha }}$$

content: Two metallic spheres of equal outer radii are found to have same moment of inertia about their respective diameters. Then, which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

options.content: The two spheres have equal masses.
The ratio of their masses is nearly 1.67 : 1.
The spheres are made of different materials.
Their rotational kinetic energies will be equal when rotated with equal uniform angular speed about their respective diameters.

explanation:
As, inner radii are not given, so we cannot calculate their masses. Also, it is not given about the nature of material of spheres.

The rotational kinetic energy of sphere,

$${(KE)_{rot}} = {1 \over 2}I{\omega ^2}$$

where, I = moment of inertia

and $$\omega $$ = angular speed.

It is given that, the two metallic spheres have same moment of inertia (I) about their respective diameters. So, their rotational kinetic energies will be equal when rotated with equal uniform angular speed ($$\omega $$) about their respective diameters.

content: A simple pendulum of length l is displaced, so that its taught string is horizontal and then released. A uniform bar pivoted at one end is simultaneously released from its horizontal position. If their motions are synchronous, what is the length of the bar?
explanation:
The velocity of the particle at extreme position.

$$v = \sqrt {2gh} $$ ..........[from $${v^2} - {u^2} = 2gh$$ ]

Let when released from extreme position, the pendulum traverse an angular distance of $$\theta $$ as shown below.


Here, h = l sin$$\theta $$

Then, linear velocity, $$v = \sqrt {2gl\sin \theta } $$

and angular velocity, $${\omega _1} = {v \over l} = {{\sqrt {2gl\sin \theta } } \over l}$$

= $${{\sqrt {2g\sin \theta } } \over l}$$

For a bar moved through the same angular distance $$\theta $$ as shown below.


Here, distance travelled, $$h = {{l'} \over 2}\sin \theta $$ , where l' is the length of bar.

Potential energy = Rotational kinetic energy

$$mgh = {1 \over 2}I\omega _2^2$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$mg{{l'} \over 2}\sin \theta = {1 \over 2}{{m{{(l')}^2}} \over 3} \times \omega _2^2$$ [$$ \because $$ for rod, $$I = {{m{l^2}} \over 3}$$]

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$gl'\sin \theta = {{{{(l')}^2}} \over 3}\omega _2^2$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${\omega _2} = \sqrt {{{3gl'\sin \theta } \over {{{(l')}^2}}}} = \sqrt {{{3g\sin \theta } \over {l'}}} $$

As the motions of pendulum and bar are synchoronous, so their angular velocities will be equal, i.e.

$${\omega _1}$$ = $${\omega _2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\sqrt {{{2g\sin \theta } \over l}} $$ = $$\sqrt {{{3g\sin \theta } \over l'}} $$

$${2 \over l} = {3 \over {l'}} \Rightarrow l' = {3 \over 2}l$$

$$ \therefore $$ The length of the bar is $${3 \over 2}l$$ .
